title: Magnetic quantum phases of spin-orbit-coupled anisotropic dipolar bosons in square lattices
abstract: We examine the two-dimensional spin-orbit-coupled bosons in the presence of an anisotropic dipolar interaction in square lattices. The spin-orbit coupling leads to finite-momentum superfluid and supersolid states, while the nearest-neighbour interaction induces crystalline characteristics in the quantum phases of soft-core bosons. We employ site-decoupled Gutzwiller ansatz and mean-field decoupling theory to obtain the phase diagrams and investigate the effects of the tilt of magnetic dipoles with respect to the polarization axis. Our study reveals the intriguing quantum phase transition of checkerboard finite-momentum phase-twisted and phase-stripe states into their stripe counterparts at a magic tilt angle, at which the off-site interaction along one of the directions becomes zero. At smaller tilt angles, the checkerboard charge-density-wave phase intervened by two compressible finite-momentum phases, and at strong spin-orbit coupling strengths, the phase-twisted supersolid and superfluid phases emerge. At larger tilt angles, a transition between the striped order of phase-twisted states and phase-stripe states occurs. The inclusion of off-site inter-component correlation leads to density-correlated phases, lattice-induced supersolid, and ferromagnetic quantum phases. Our study highlights novel finite-momentum crystal phases of spin-orbit-coupled dipolar bosons and provides a parameter space to observe them in quantum gas experiments.
summary: This paper explores how the combination of spin-orbit coupling and tunable anisotropic dipolar interactions creates complex quantum phases in 2D square lattices. By varying the tilt angle of the dipoles, the researchers found that the system can transition between checkerboard and stripe-ordered states, particularly at a specific 'magic angle'. The study also highlights the emergence of phase-twisted and phase-striped supersolids. These findings offer a roadmap for experimentalists using ultracold atoms to observe exotic density-correlated and magnetic phases.
main_problem: Investigating the interplay between synthetic spin-orbit coupling and anisotropic dipolar interactions in 2D square optical lattices to understand the emergence of exotic magnetic quantum phases.
main_result: The study identifies novel finite-momentum crystal phases, including phase-twisted and phase-striped supersolids and superfluids, and reveals a quantum phase transition at a magic tilt angle where off-site interactions vanish.
method: The authors employ a site-decoupled Gutzwiller ansatz and mean-field decoupling theory, supplemented by second-order perturbative analysis to determine phase boundaries.
model_or_system: A two-component (pseudospinor) bosonic system in a 2D square optical lattice described by an extended Bose-Hubbard Model, including Rashba-type spin-orbit coupling and anisotropic nearest-neighbor dipolar interactions.
key_observables: Superfluid order parameter, on-site densities, density imbalance, static structure factor S(k), momentum distribution, and magnetization.
important_parameters: Spin-orbit coupling strength (gamma), dipole tilt angle (theta), hopping amplitude (J/U), dipolar interaction strength (V/U), and the magic angle (theta_M approx 35.3 degrees).
main_assumptions: The long-range dipolar interaction is truncated to nearest-neighbor interactions, and the single-particle hopping and SOC terms are treated as small perturbations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of spin-flip complex hopping and dipole orientation; other figures present ground-state phase diagrams for various SOC strengths and tilt angles, and illustrate phase boundaries for transitions between insulating and compressible phases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and model, details the theoretical methodology (Gutzwiller and mean-field), presents phase diagrams and results regarding the effects of tilt angle and SOC strength, and concludes with the implications for experimental observations.
why_interesting: It provides a detailed parameter space for observing novel finite-momentum crystal phases and supersolidity in ultracold dipolar gases, which is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and synthetic gauge field research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of spin-flip complex hopping (γ) of spin-orbit coupled dipolar bosons in two-dimensional square op- tical lattices. The dipoles are assumed to lie within the yz-plane and the angle θ subtended by the polarization axis with the z-axis is the tilt angle, that defines the direction of polarization. The tilt angle is shown by the blue-shaded region. The angle between the polariza- tion axis and the relative position vector (r4 −r1), α14, is shown by the green shaded region. The dipolar interaction strengths between bosons at lattice sites (p, q) and (p ± 1, q) are denoted by Vσσ and V↑↓for intra- and interspin interactions, whereas these interactions along...

FIG. 2. Spatial distributions of spin-resolved densities and phases in lattice supersolid states. Panels (a)−(c) show the spin-up density n↑, (d)−(f) the spin-down density n↓, and (g)−(i) the total density N = n↑+ n↓. Panels (j)−(l) display the phase θ↑of spin-up com- ponent. The three columns correspond to the phase-twisted lattice SS, phase-striped lattice SS, and zero-momentum lattice SS phases, respectively. Here a is the lattice constant.

FIG. 3. Real-space phase θσ variations across lattice and the asso- ciated spin-resolved momentum distributions for different supersolid states. Upper panel (a,b,c) depicts the phase configurations of the PT, PS, and ZM supersolid, while lower panel (d,e,f) displays the corresponding momentum-space distributions. Here, a is the lattice constant.

FIG. 5. (a) The average SF order parameter as a function of hopping amplitude at V = 0.7 and θ = 30◦for different SOC strengths, that demarcates density-modulating compressible phases on either sides of CBDW(1,0) phase. (b) The spin-dependent momentum measure characterizing PT-DCSS to PS-DCSS transition with γ. (c) Structure factor at (π, π) and (π, 0) distinguishing different orders of mag- netic supersolid phases at J = 0.08 and θ = 35.5◦. (d) ϱ with hopping amplitude for two cases corresponding to PT-SF to PS-SF (green solid line) and PT-SSS to PS-SSS (red dashed line) phase tran- sition.

FIG. 4. Ground-state phase diagrams for SOC strengths (a) γ = 0, (b) γ = 0.02, (c) γ = 0.04 and γ = 0.1 with ζ = 0. The solid (dashed) lines are the phase boundaries for the tilt angle θ = 0 ◦(30 ◦) in the hopping amplitude and dipolar interaction strength plane. The red filled (open) circles are the phase boundaries between incom- pressible and compressible phases, obtained by mean-field decou- pling theory for θ = 0 ◦(30 ◦) [Eq. (12)]. Here, U↑↓/U = 0.5 and µ/J = 10.

FIG. 7. Ground-state phase diagrams for U↑↓/U = 0.5 and µ/J = 10 with SOC strengths (a) γ = 0, (b) 0.02, (c) 0.04 and (d) 0.1. The tilt angle is θ = 45 ◦and ζ = 0. Phase boundaries marked by red filled circles are obtained from analytical theory [Eq. (15)].

FIG. 6. Ground-state phase diagrams for U↑↓/U = 0.5 and µ/J = 10 with SOC strengths (a) γ = 0, (b) 0.02, (c) 0.04, and (d) 0.1. The tilt angle of polarized dipolar bosons is θ = 35.5 ◦with ζ = 0. Phase boundaries marked by red filled circles are obtained from analytical mean-field decoupling theory [Eq. (15)].

FIG. 8. Ground-state phase diagrams in presence of interspin NN interactions V↑↓= ζV where ζ = 1 for U↑↓/U = 0.5 and µ/J = 10 with SOC strengths γ = 0, 0.02, and 0.1 in (a,d), (b,e), and (c,f). The tilt angle is θ = 0 ◦for upper panel (a,b,c) and 30 ◦for lower panel (d,e,f). The phase boundaries marked by the red and blue filled circles are obtained from mean-field decoupling theory [Eq. (16)].

FIG. 9. (a) Variation of the magnetization M and SF-order param- eter imbalance ∆ϕ as a function of the dipolar interaction V/U for θ = 30◦, γ = 0, and J = 0.07U. Different quantum phases such as ZM-SF, zFM, ZM-LSS, and cDW(2, 0) are identified, with phase boundaries indicated by shaded regions. (b) Corresponding behavior of the structure factors S(0, π), S(π, π), and SF-order parameter ϕ as a function of V/U for θ = 30◦, γ = 0.1, and J = 0.025U. The emergence of PT-SF, PT-SSS, PT-LSS, and cDW(1, 0) phases is highlighted.

FIG. 10. Ground-state phase diagrams in presence of interspin NN interactions V↑↓= ζV where ζ = 1 for U12/U = 0.5 and µ/J = 10 with SOC strengths γ = 0, 0.02 and 0.1 in (a) & (d), (b) & (e) and (c) & (f). The angle is θ = 35.5 ◦from (a)-(c) and 45 ◦from (d)-(f). The cSDW phase possesses the stripe-order in total occupancy while SDW phase has stripe-ordered variation in individ- ual components. Phase boundaries marked by red filled circles are obtained from mean-field decoupling theory [Eq. (15)].
